title: Quiz: Athermality and Quantum Thermodynamics
description: Test your understanding of the resource theory of thermodynamics

questions.question: In the resource theory of thermodynamics, what are the free states?

options.text: Pure energy eigenstates
Thermal (Gibbs) states γ = e^{-βH}/Z
States with maximum entropy
Any diagonal state in the energy basis

questions.explanation: The free states are thermal (Gibbs) states at the bath temperature: γ = e^{-βH}/Z. These are the states that can be prepared 'for free' by equilibrating with the heat bath.

questions.question: The non-equilibrium free energy F(ρ) = ⟨H⟩ - TS(ρ) serves as a resource monotone because:

options.text: It always increases under thermal operations
It never increases under thermal operations
It remains constant under all operations
It equals zero for all states

questions.explanation: The non-equilibrium free energy is a monotone because it never increases under thermal operations. This is the resource-theoretic statement of the second law of thermodynamics.

questions.question: The maximum extractable work from state ρ at temperature T is bounded by:

options.text: The energy ⟨H⟩ of the state
The entropy S(ρ) of the state
The free energy difference F(ρ) - F(γ)
The temperature T times entropy

questions.explanation: The maximum extractable work is bounded by W_max ≤ F(ρ) - F(γ) = (1/β)D(ρ||γ), which is the athermality of the state. This represents the 'distance' from thermal equilibrium.

questions.question: Thermal operations are characterized by:

options.text: Any unitary evolution on the system
Energy-conserving unitaries coupling system to thermal bath
Operations that increase system temperature
Measurements in the energy basis only

questions.explanation: Thermal operations are quantum channels that can be implemented by coupling the system to a thermal bath via an energy-conserving unitary [U, H_S + H_B] = 0, then tracing out the bath.

questions.question: A pure excited state |1⟩ has more athermality (extractable work) than the ground state |0⟩ at finite temperature.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: True. At finite temperature, the thermal state has non-zero population in both levels. The excited state |1⟩ is further from thermal equilibrium than the ground state |0⟩, hence has more athermality and extractable work.

questions.question: What is the role of quantum coherence in the energy basis for thermodynamics?

options.text: It has no thermodynamic significance
It destroys all work extraction capability
It provides a purely quantum contribution to free energy
It makes the state thermal

questions.explanation: Coherence in the energy basis provides a purely quantum contribution to the free energy: F(ρ) = F_classical(ρ_diag) + T·C_rel(ρ). This quantum contribution can enable advantages in certain thermodynamic protocols.

questions.question: Thermo-majorization determines:

options.text: The temperature of a thermal state
Whether one state can be transformed to another under thermal operations
The maximum entropy of a system
The energy spectrum of the Hamiltonian

questions.explanation: Thermo-majorization provides the complete criterion for when state ρ can be transformed to σ under thermal operations. It generalizes standard majorization to include temperature effects.

questions.question: Which statements about the resource theory of thermodynamics are correct? (Select all that apply)

options.text: The second law is encoded as monotonicity of free energy
Carnot efficiency is achievable in quantum heat engines
Athermality equals the relative entropy D(ρ||γ) times β⁻¹
Thermal operations can create athermality from thermal states

questions.explanation: (a) Correct: The second law states F(E(ρ)) ≤ F(ρ) for thermal operations. (b) Correct: Carnot efficiency is achievable in the asymptotic limit with reversible protocols. (c) Correct: Athermality is defined as ΔF = β⁻¹D(ρ||γ). (d) Incorrect: Thermal operations preserve thermal states and cannot create athermality from them.
